scoreSemantics.headline: An x/10 score is a measured rollup at the current scorer resolution, not a claim of arcade-perfect behavior.
scoreSemantics.tenOutOfTen: 10/10 means no known measured gap under the current scorer and evidence coverage. It remains a guardrail pass until broader reference, expert-play, and edge-case evidence says otherwise.
scoreSemantics.confidence: Confidence estimates how much trust to place in the score as a release signal.
scoreSemantics.resolution: Resolution describes how fine-grained the scorer currently is and which blind spots may remain.

releaseGate.Notes: Full score refresh after all major cycles
Split from generic UI shell before final gate

priorityRows.metric: Audio identity, event feedback, and cue alignment

explanation.calculation: Release audio score blends cue identity, reference spectral similarity, reference-window precision, overlap, and event alignment from audio cue comparison artifacts.
explanation.grounding: Best-case grounding comes from labeled Galaga-family reference audio clips, segmented Aurora runtime captures, cue/event logs, waveform and spectral measurements, and per-cue gap analysis.
explanation.meaning: Players hear whether shots, hits, explosions, boss damage, capture, rescue, and challenge results communicate the right event at the right moment. Designers use it to protect feedback clarity and arcade identity.

scoreContext.resolution: 21 cue/event comparisons with waveform, spectral, overlap, and alignment features
scoreContext.scoreMeaning: Material conformance gap with meaningful user-experience or reference-identity impact.

priorityRows.why: Largest current score gap and high user-experience impact: shots, explosions, boss damage, challenge results, capture/rescue feedback.
priorityRows.next: Run a longer audio segmentation/model-assisted analysis cycle.

explanation.calculation: Level arc is read from the level-arc conformance report, combining stage distinctiveness, challenge-stage identity, later-stage complexity, pressure curve, reward/rescue layering, and learning/mastery windows.
explanation.grounding: Best-case grounding is multi-stage reference evidence plus Aurora harness windows for stage families, challenge layers, stage signatures, pressure/loss windows, and persona progression.
explanation.meaning: Players feel whether the game grows, teaches, surprises, and rewards mastery over time. Designers use it to detect repetition, flat difficulty, or escalation without reward.

scoreContext.resolution: multi-submetric level-arc report with stage families, challenge layers, pressure, reward, and persona evidence
scoreContext.scoreMeaning: Good conformance, but the gap is likely visible to attentive players or designers in some scenarios.

priorityRows.why: Controls whether long play feels like Galaga-like escalation rather than repeated pressure.
priorityRows.next: Apply one narrow Stage 12 reward candidate and rerun the frozen conformance loop.

priorityRows.metric: Overall visual look and feel: gameplay, start page, typography complexity

explanation.calculation: Current score is an estimated planning value until a dedicated visual conformance scorer lands; it is informed by UI shell checks, screenshots, contact sheets, and known visual debt.
explanation.grounding: Best-case grounding will compare reference and Aurora contact sheets across start, attract, gameplay, score, popup, and game-over surfaces with palette, typography, density, sprite readability, and layout checks.
explanation.meaning: Players decide at a glance whether the game feels like a polished arcade object. Designers use it to align readability, theme, typography, and visual hierarchy before subjective tuning.

scoreContext.resolution: estimated planning score; visual scorer not yet promoted
scoreContext.scoreMeaning: Material conformance gap with meaningful user-experience or reference-identity impact.

priorityRows.status: Estimated; needs dedicated visual conformance scorer
priorityRows.why: A high score can still feel off if start text, density, contrast, alien readability, and arcade typography do not cohere.
priorityRows.next: Create a visual-look scorer covering start/attract page, gameplay readability, typography density, color discipline, and reference contact sheets.
priorityRows.evidence: UI shell suite plus generated frame/contact-sheet artifacts

priorityRows.metric: Stage 4 pressure exact replay / pressure curve precision

explanation.calculation: Score is the current weakest level-arc pressure submetric, focused on whether known pressure/loss windows reproduce as exact or same-window replay events under controlled harness runs.
explanation.grounding: Best-case grounding is source pressure/loss windows, frozen seeds, replayable input paths, collision diagnostics, and repeated current-vs-source pressure curve comparisons.
explanation.meaning: Players should feel pressure that is learnable and fair, not random. Designers use it to tune threat density, dodge lanes, and failure recovery without creating arbitrary deaths.

scoreContext.resolution: narrow pressure/loss replay windows; exact replay coverage still limited
scoreContext.scoreMeaning: Material conformance gap with meaningful user-experience or reference-identity impact.

priorityRows.why: Pressure should be learnable and reproducible, not merely present in one run.
priorityRows.effort: Medium-high; prior runs ~12.8 min wall / 18.5 min CPU
priorityRows.next: Run focused source-window replay matching after the Stage 12 loop validates candidate mechanics.

priorityRows.metric: Alien entry to levels: formation, timing, and methods

explanation.calculation: Composite proxy: 45% stage-opening timing fidelity, 35% stage-opening geometry fidelity, and 20% movement-grammar expansion until alien entry is promoted to its own scorer.
explanation.grounding: Best-case grounding will use reference and Aurora stage-entry contact sheets, rack timing traces, path-family labels, formation geometry, and early/mid/late level comparisons.
explanation.meaning: Players read the whole level from the first entry pattern. Designers use it to make stages feel authored, recognizable, and increasingly sophisticated before combat fully starts.

scoreContext.resolution: composite proxy from opening timing, geometry, and movement grammar
scoreContext.scoreMeaning: Strong measured conformance with known remaining risk mostly in narrower edge cases, coverage, or polish.

priorityRows.status: Composite proxy: stage opening timing + geometry + movement grammar
priorityRows.why: Entry formations and rack timing are a first-order arcade authenticity signal before combat even starts.
priorityRows.next: Promote alien-entry as its own scored submetric; compare stage-entry contact sheets and timing traces across early/mid/late levels.

priorityRows.metric: Challenge-stage variation and new alien/formations introduction

explanation.calculation: Composite proxy: 45% challenge timing fidelity, 35% challenge-stage identity, and 20% long-run non-repetition until a dedicated challenge-variation scorer exists.
explanation.grounding: Best-case grounding is reference challenge-stage footage, alien/path family labels, bonus opportunity windows, result feedback timing, and Aurora stage-to-stage variation traces.
explanation.meaning: Players should experience challenge stages as learnable bonus set pieces that introduce new motion and scoring opportunities. Designers use it to prevent bonus rounds from becoming repetitive pauses.

scoreContext.resolution: composite proxy from challenge timing, challenge identity, and non-repetition
scoreContext.scoreMeaning: Strong measured conformance with known remaining risk mostly in narrower edge cases, coverage, or polish.

priorityRows.status: Composite proxy: challenge timing + challenge identity + non-repetition
priorityRows.why: Challenge stages should teach new motion/reward patterns, not only pause normal combat.
priorityRows.next: Add a challenge-variation metric for alien type introduction, path families, result feedback, and bonus opportunity clarity.

explanation.calculation: Score is read from the progression/persona quality category, including persona safety checks, stage ordering, and whether different skill profiles see an appropriate ramp.
explanation.grounding: Best-case grounding comes from controlled persona runs, stage snapshots, loss/recovery traces, and reference-informed expectations for learning, mastery, and escalation.
explanation.meaning: Players should feel the game becoming harder for understandable reasons. Designers use it to keep novice, advanced, and expert experiences coherent across a long session.

scoreContext.resolution: scorer-backed artifact with selected harness windows
scoreContext.scoreMeaning: Good conformance, but the gap is likely visible to attentive players or designers in some scenarios.

priorityRows.why: Keeps the game learnable across skill levels and supports later-stage quality.
priorityRows.next: Resolve remaining ordering edge case after higher-value audio/level-arc work.

explanation.calculation: Score is read from the stage-1 opening timing category, comparing measured Aurora event timing against reference opening-window timing metrics and tolerances.
explanation.grounding: Best-case grounding is a canonical stage-1 reference window with event timestamps, Aurora controlled-clock captures, and delta reports for first entry, arrival, and first dive timing.
explanation.meaning: Players form their first feel judgment in the opening seconds. Designers use it to lock the initial rhythm before tuning deeper complexity.

scoreContext.resolution: scorer-backed artifact with selected harness windows
scoreContext.scoreMeaning: Good conformance, but the gap is likely visible to attentive players or designers in some scenarios.

priorityRows.why: First impression and direct reference feel.
priorityRows.next: Tune only after audio and level-arc priorities unless regressions appear.

explanation.calculation: Current score uses the UI shell quality category as a proxy until the frame gets its own arcade-style rubric.
explanation.grounding: Best-case grounding will score cabinet rails, bezel proportions, button density, labels, build/date treatment, chroming, responsive fit, and visual consistency across local/dev/beta/prod surfaces.
explanation.meaning: Players experience the frame as the cabinet around every game. Designers use it to make the platform feel trustworthy, arcade-native, and not like a generic web wrapper.

scoreContext.resolution: UI shell proxy; dedicated visual/modal rubric still needed
scoreContext.scoreMeaning: Strong measured conformance with known remaining risk mostly in narrower edge cases, coverage, or polish.

priorityRows.status: Measured as UI shell; needs separate arcade-frame style rubric
priorityRows.why: The cabinet frame is the constant product surface around every game.
priorityRows.next: Split frame style from generic shell integrity: rails, bezel density, labels, chroming, build/date treatment.

explanation.calculation: Current score uses the UI shell suite as a proxy until help, score, account, feedback, leaderboard, and game-over modals get a modal-specific scorer.
explanation.grounding: Best-case grounding will compare each modal surface for layout, typography, arcade tone, score clarity, keyboard/controller ergonomics, and no-overlap responsive behavior.
explanation.meaning: Players rely on these screens to understand scoring, recover from a run, file feedback, and trust records. Designers use it to keep utility surfaces polished without breaking arcade immersion.

scoreContext.resolution: UI shell proxy; dedicated visual/modal rubric still needed
scoreContext.scoreMeaning: Strong measured conformance with known remaining risk mostly in narrower edge cases, coverage, or polish.

priorityRows.status: Measured through UI shell suite; needs modal-specific scoring
priorityRows.why: Popup surfaces carry learning, scoring trust, feedback, and player records.
priorityRows.next: Add modal-specific scorer for help, scoring, feedback, account, leaderboard, and game-over result screens.

explanation.calculation: Score is read from the dive-safety quality category and associated harness checks for unfair collision, lane, and persona safety regressions.
explanation.grounding: Best-case grounding is repeated persona/seed sweeps, collision windows, near-miss traces, and pressure diagnostics after every risky movement or threat change.
explanation.meaning: Players accept hard deaths when they feel earned. Designers use it as a guardrail so added pressure does not become unfairness.

scoreContext.resolution: seed/persona safety guardrails and pressure-sensitive collision checks
scoreContext.scoreMeaning: Strong measured conformance with known remaining risk mostly in narrower edge cases, coverage, or polish.

priorityRows.why: Protects user trust while pressure is increased.
priorityRows.effort: Guardrail; 30-90 min per risky gameplay cycle
priorityRows.next: Keep as required guardrail for pressure/reward changes.

explanation.calculation: Score is read from the player-movement correspondence category, comparing movement traces and control response against the reference-derived movement target.
explanation.grounding: Best-case grounding is reference movement traces, Aurora controlled input traces, speed/position deltas, and regression checks across viewport and persona modes.
explanation.meaning: Players feel this as the basic trust in the ship. Designers use it as a do-not-regress foundation for every other gameplay improvement.

scoreContext.resolution: reference trace plus controlled movement harness checks; expert micro-feel can still exceed scorer resolution
scoreContext.scoreMeaning: No known measured gap under the current scorer and evidence coverage. This is a guardrail pass, not proof of perfection.

priorityRows.why: Core control feel is already excellent.
priorityRows.next: Do not tune unless a new reference metric proves a gap.

explanation.calculation: Score is read from the combat-responsiveness category, covering shot timing, hit registration, close-shot behavior, and event feedback guardrails.
explanation.grounding: Best-case grounding is controlled shot/hit scenarios, close-contact tests, event logs, and paired visual/audio feedback timing.
explanation.meaning: Players need shots and impacts to feel immediate and legible. Designers use it to protect core combat feel while improving explosions and sound semantics.

scoreContext.resolution: functional combat-response guardrails; audiovisual semantics are scored separately
scoreContext.scoreMeaning: No known measured gap under the current scorer and evidence coverage. This is a guardrail pass, not proof of perfection.

priorityRows.why: Core combat response is already excellent.

explanation.calculation: Score is read from the stage-1 opening geometry category, comparing formation layout and opening positions against the reference geometry target.
explanation.grounding: Best-case grounding is reference contact sheets, Aurora opening captures, formation coordinate traces, and geometry tolerance checks.
explanation.meaning: Players read formation authenticity before they consciously notice details. Designers use it as the locked baseline for alien-entry and formation work.

scoreContext.resolution: opening formation geometry checks; later-stage entry variation is separate
scoreContext.scoreMeaning: No known measured gap under the current scorer and evidence coverage. This is a guardrail pass, not proof of perfection.

explanation.calculation: Score is read from the capture/rescue quality category, including capture, no-leak, rescue, and rule-boundary harness checks.
explanation.grounding: Best-case grounding is reference capture/rescue behavior, controlled Aurora scenarios, event logs, state assertions, and score/reward feedback traces.
explanation.meaning: Players see capture and rescue as a signature Galaga risk/reward mechanic. Designers use it as a hard identity guardrail while improving feedback and scoring opportunities.

scoreContext.resolution: rule/state harness checks; feedback clarity and reward feel are separate
scoreContext.scoreMeaning: No known measured gap under the current scorer and evidence coverage. This is a guardrail pass, not proof of perfection.

priorityRows.why: Strong Galaga identity mechanic; should not regress while feedback improves.
priorityRows.next: Use as release blocker for capture/rescue-adjacent audio or explosion changes.

explanation.calculation: Score is read from the challenge-stage timing category, comparing Aurora challenge entry, transition, result, and timing metrics against reference tolerances.
explanation.grounding: Best-case grounding is reference challenge-stage timing windows, Aurora controlled-clock scenarios, result feedback traces, and pass/fail delta reports.
explanation.meaning: Players need bonus stages to feel rhythmic and fair. Designers use this as the timing guardrail while adding more challenge-stage variety.

scoreContext.resolution: challenge timing deltas within tolerance; variation and teaching value are separate
scoreContext.scoreMeaning: Strong measured conformance with known remaining risk mostly in narrower edge cases, coverage, or polish.

priorityRows.why: Timing is strong; variation is the gap, not baseline timing.
priorityRows.next: Preserve while adding challenge variation scoring.

newFirstClassAxes: Alien entry to levels: formation layout, timing, path method, and whether different stages enter differently.
Challenge-stage variation: new alien types, new entry formations/styles, path families, reward/result feedback, and teaching value.
Overall visual look and feel: gameplay readability, start/attract typography density, copy complexity, color discipline, and reference contact sheets.
Arcade console frame UI: cabinet frame, bezel/rails, build/date trust signals, button density, and arcade-style containment.
Popup/help/scoring surfaces: help, scoring, leaderboard, account, feedback, and game-over result formatting as their own modal-quality family.
maintenanceRules: Refresh this artifact after each full quality score, investment-priority run, or major conformance loop.

Before a serious /dev, /beta, or /production release candidate, refresh npm run harness:analyze:conformance-economics and npm run harness:build:release-conformance-dashboard so release docs include conformance, resource/time, chart, past-goal, and next-goal reads.
Any long-cycle local compute or model/API/GPU-assisted assessment should be wrapped with npm run harness:measure and declared with its axis and resource classes.
Keep the local dashboard generated from this artifact data; do not link or publish it through player-facing Platinum surfaces until explicitly approved.
Treat rows marked estimated/composite as measurement debt: useful for planning, but not release-proof until backed by a harness.
Keep user-facing release gates separate from harness-learning wins. A rejected candidate still belongs in artifacts when it teaches the loop what not to keep.
Prefer work with a large score gap, high user-experience impact, reusable ingestion/harness value, and clear guardrails.
